analysis.lecture_title: Pressure and Free-Surface Shape in Accelerating and Rotating Fluids
analysis.lecture_summary: The lecture analyzes how body accelerations (linear acceleration of a container and rotation about an axis) change hydrostatic pressure distributions and deform free surfaces. It shows how to set up force balances on fluid elements in accelerating frames, derives the slope and shape of free surfaces (inclined plane for translation, paraboloid for rotation), and uses volume and pressure balance to compute depths, submerged volumes, and conditions for overflow or stability. Practical topics include using cylindrical coordinates for elemental force balances and unit considerations for volumetric flow.
core_topics: Parabolic free-surface in a rotating fluid
Force balance on fluid elements (Cartesian and cylindrical)
Volume/height relations for inclined free surfaces
Use of cylindrical coordinates for pressure element
learning_objectives: Explain how constant linear acceleration modifies hydrostatic pressure and free-surface orientation
Derive the pressure gradient and free-surface slope for a container accelerating horizontally
Derive the parabolic free-surface shape for a fluid in steady rotation
Apply elemental force balances to obtain pressure distributions in Cartesian and cylindrical coordinates
Compute depths, submerged volumes, and conditions for overflow or stability from geometric and volume constraints
Convert and reason about common volumetric flow units
assumed_prerequisites: Newtonian mechanics in non-inertial frames (accelerating reference frames)
Familiarity with cylindrical coordinates and differential elements
